title: Quick check, queue versus ring group

questions.prompt: A four-person after-hours support rota wants the on-call engineer to ring first; the backup engineer should start ringing 15 seconds later and stop after another 30 seconds. If nobody answers, the call goes to voicemail. Which configuration fits?
choices: Ring group with Custom ring strategy and per-member delay/timeout
Ring group with Ring Sequentially strategy
questions.explanation: Ring group Custom strategy gives per-member Delay (when they start ringing) and Ring Timeout (when they stop). This is the right shape for staggered rotas. A queue would be overkill; Ring Sequentially doesn't support the overlap pattern (member 2 ringing alongside member 1).

questions.prompt: Which of the following are true differences between a queue and a ring group? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Queues support dynamic agents who can log in and out; ring group membership is fixed
Queues produce wait-time and answered-rate reports; ring groups don't
Queues support Music on Hold and periodic announcements while callers wait; ring groups don't
Ring groups support more ring strategies than queues
Ring groups can have failover destinations; queues can't
questions.explanation: Queues add dynamic agents, reports, and waiting-caller prompts. Both have failover destinations. Queues support more strategies than ring groups (Least Recent, Fewest Calls, Skill-Based, Round Robin Memory), not fewer.
